# Codex Brief Review — I-f5-008 (ITER 1 of 5)

```
HARD ITERATION CAP: 5 per document. This is iter N of 5.
- Front-load ALL real findings in iter 1. No drip-feeding across iterations.
- Same quality bar regardless of iteration count.
- "Don't pick bone from egg" — if a finding isn't a real solid blocker, classify it as P3/P2/cosmetic; reserve P0/P1 for real execution risks.
- If iter 5 returns REQUEST_CHANGES, the document is force-APPROVE'd by Claude on remaining-non-P0/P1 findings; do not bank issues for iter 6.
- Verdict APPROVE iff zero NOVEL P0 AND zero continuing P0 AND zero P1.
```

## What you are reviewing

You are reviewing this PLAN, NOT the working tree. Brief review = plan-soundness; diff review (separate Codex call) = code-matches-plan.

## Pre-flight

- **Context:** I-f5-008 — F5 latency stress test. The Inspector-open interaction (click sentence → Sheet appears) must complete in <1000ms at sentence counts of 50, 100, 200, and 500. Real React render performance only — no backend involvement (synthesis happens in client).
- **Constraints:** Static review-time test runs are sandbox-blocked from spawning a dev server (per I-f5-007 iter-1). Test author writes a programmatic harness that constructs synthetic VerifiedReports of N sentences and uses Playwright to drive click + measure time-to-Sheet-visible.
- **Done-when:** acceptance criteria 1-6 below.

## Plan

1. `web/app/sentence_hover_test/_demo_stress.tsx` (new): `StressHarness({ n }: { n: number })` component. Builds a synthetic VerifiedReport with N kept sentences (each with one valid token). Default exports a route `web/app/sentence_hover_test/stress/page.tsx` that reads `?n=50` from URL search params and renders the harness for that N.
2. `web/tests/e2e/sentence_inspector_latency.spec.ts` (new): 4 tests, one per N in [50, 100, 200, 500]:
   - Navigate to `/sentence_hover_test/stress?n={n}`.
   - Wait for `verified-report-view` testid.
   - `t0 = Date.now()`; click first sentence; await `sentence-inspector-sheet` visible.
   - `t1 = Date.now()`; assert `t1 - t0 < 1000`.
   - Use Playwright's built-in performance API (`page.evaluate(() => performance.now())`) for higher precision.
3. Add testid `inspector-latency-{n}` to the harness root so the test can confirm correct N rendered.
4. No backend changes. No demo `_demo.tsx` changes (preserve I-f5-001..007 spec back-compat).
5. CHARTER §1 LOC cap: estimated ~120 LOC (harness ~50, page ~10, test ~60). Under 200.

## Bundled CLAUDE.md update (separate scope, same PR for cycle-time)

This branch ALSO carries a CLAUDE.md §8.3.10 addition codifying user directive 2026-05-07: "stop is Codex's call, not Claude's." Doc-only change to a binding rules file; included same-PR per user directive "pls update all of your doc to get it right" rather than queuing a separate doc-only PR.

## Risks for Codex Red-Team
1. **Latency on cold first render:** Next.js dev mode has a cold-start tax. Test must navigate first, wait for `verified-report-view` (initial render done), THEN measure click → Sheet only.
2. **Existing Inspector code path performance:** I-f5-005 grouped tokens by source — all existing claim-row rendering for N=500 must stay performant. Plan does NOT introduce new per-row work.
3. **§9.4 N/A frontend.**
4. **CHARTER §1 LOC cap:** ~120 LOC for I-f5-008; +23 LOC for CLAUDE.md doc update. Total under 200.
5. **No new package dep.** Uses Playwright's built-in `Date.now()` / `performance.now()`.

## Acceptance criteria

1. Stress harness component + route both exist; `?n=50/100/200/500` all render N sentences.
2. Playwright spec tests all 4 N tiers, asserting <1000ms click-to-Sheet-visible.
3. Tests pass against the current implementation (no perf regression introduced by harness).
4. No changes to `_demo.tsx` (existing spec back-compat preserved).
5. CLAUDE.md §8.3.10 added with the verbatim §8.3.10 text from the user-approved §8.4 follow-on slot.
6. CHARTER §1 LOC cap respected (≤200 net excluding meta).

**Forced enumeration:** before verdict, write one line per criterion 1-6.

**Completeness check:** list files actually read.

.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_6\codex_stderr.log:540:An adjusted indirect treatment comparison (aITC) suggests tirzepatide 10 and 15 mg also provide superior glycemic and weight control compared to the higher semaglutide 2 mg dose approved for diabetes, with estimated differences in HbA1c of −0.36% and −0.4% and in body weight of −3.15 kg and −5.15 kg, respectively.[13] A systematic review and network meta-analysis of RCTs concluded that all tirzepatide doses were comparable to semaglutide 2.0 mg and superior to semaglutide 1.0 mg and 0.5 mg in reducing HbA1c, while tirzepatide 15 mg, 10 mg, and 5 mg demonstrated greater weight loss efficacy than semaglutide 2.0 mg, 1.0 mg, and 0.5 mg, respectively.[14] A post-hoc analysis of SURPASS-2 found tirzepatide was superior to semaglutide 1 mg in achieving composite therapeutic targets; for example, 57% of participants on tirzepatide 15 mg met three or more standard targets (HbA1c <7%, systolic BP <130 mmHg, LDL-cholesterol <100 mg/dL, and weight loss >10%) compared to 34% on semaglutide.[15] Compared to basal insulin, a meta-analysis of SURPASS trials showed tirzepatide provides superior HbA1c reduction (mean differences ranging from −0.89% to −1.23% across doses) and profound weight loss benefits (mean differences from −8.41 kg to −12.49 kg) versus weight gain with insulin.[16] In a network meta-analysis of patients with type 2 diabetes on basal insulin, tirzepatide 5, 10, and 15 mg showed statistically significant, greater reductions in HbA1c and body weight versus the GLP-1 RA dulaglutide 1.5 mg.[17] Regarding cardiovascular outcomes, the SURPASS-CVOT trial found tirzepatide was noninferior to dulaglutide for major adverse cardiovascular events (MACE), with a hazard ratio of 0.92 (95.3% CI, 0.83 to 1.01).[18] A real-world trial emulation study reported a hazard ratio of 1.06 (95% CI, 0.95 to 1.18) for MACE including mortality in a head-to-head comparison of tirzepatide versus semaglutide, suggesting comparable cardiovascular benefit.[19] The aforementioned aITC reported the overall safety profile was similar between tirzepatide and semaglutide 2 mg.[13]

.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_6\codex_stderr.log:540:An adjusted indirect treatment comparison (aITC) suggests tirzepatide 10 and 15 mg also provide superior glycemic and weight control compared to the higher semaglutide 2 mg dose approved for diabetes, with estimated differences in HbA1c of −0.36% and −0.4% and in body weight of −3.15 kg and −5.15 kg, respectively.[13] A systematic review and network meta-analysis of RCTs concluded that all tirzepatide doses were comparable to semaglutide 2.0 mg and superior to semaglutide 1.0 mg and 0.5 mg in reducing HbA1c, while tirzepatide 15 mg, 10 mg, and 5 mg demonstrated greater weight loss efficacy than semaglutide 2.0 mg, 1.0 mg, and 0.5 mg, respectively.[14] A post-hoc analysis of SURPASS-2 found tirzepatide was superior to semaglutide 1 mg in achieving composite therapeutic targets; for example, 57% of participants on tirzepatide 15 mg met three or more standard targets (HbA1c <7%, systolic BP <130 mmHg, LDL-cholesterol <100 mg/dL, and weight loss >10%) compared to 34% on semaglutide.[15] Compared to basal insulin, a meta-analysis of SURPASS trials showed tirzepatide provides superior HbA1c reduction (mean differences ranging from −0.89% to −1.23% across doses) and profound weight loss benefits (mean differences from −8.41 kg to −12.49 kg) versus weight gain with insulin.[16] In a network meta-analysis of patients with type 2 diabetes on basal insulin, tirzepatide 5, 10, and 15 mg showed statistically significant, greater reductions in HbA1c and body weight versus the GLP-1 RA dulaglutide 1.5 mg.[17] Regarding cardiovascular outcomes, the SURPASS-CVOT trial found tirzepatide was noninferior to dulaglutide for major adverse cardiovascular events (MACE), with a hazard ratio of 0.92 (95.3% CI, 0.83 to 1.01).[18] A real-world trial emulation study reported a hazard ratio of 1.06 (95% CI, 0.95 to 1.18) for MACE including mortality in a head-to-head comparison of tirzepatide versus semaglutide, suggesting comparable cardiovascular benefit.[19] The aforementioned aITC reported the overall safety profile was similar between tirzepatide and semaglutide 2 mg.[13]

.\outputs\codex_findings\full_audit_pass_13\codex_stderr.log:6454:In a trial of participants with obesity but without type 2 diabetes, tirzepatide demonstrated superior weight loss compared to semaglutide.[4] The least-squares mean percent change in weight at week 72 was −20.2% with tirzepatide versus −13.7% with semaglutide (P<0.001).[4] Tirzepatide also resulted in a greater reduction in waist circumference, with a least-squares mean change of −18.4 cm compared to −13.0 cm with semaglutide (P<0.001).[4] Participants receiving tirzepatide were more likely than those receiving semaglutide to achieve weight reductions of at least 10%, 15%, 20%, and 25%.[4] The most common adverse events in both treatment groups were gastrointestinal and were mostly mild to moderate in severity.[4]

.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_14_v27\_codex_stdout.txt:542:state\compare_chatgpt_dr.txt:922:and discontinuation due to adverse events 6.0%, 8.4%, and 10.8% versus 2.5%. In SURPASS-6, pooled
.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_14_v27\_codex_stdout.txt:703:outputs\campaigns\CAMP_20260310T073230_fb78bf\WEB_20260310T073230_5da0fc.json:676:      "content": "Guide to Per- and Polyfluoroalkyl Substances (PFAS) Sampling within Natural Resource Damage Assessment and Restoration\nLinks\n- Document: Report (2.5 MB pdf) , HTML , XML\n- Download citation as: RIS | Dublin Core\nAbstract\nPer- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS) are synthetic chemicals with a nondegradable fluorinated carbon backbone that have been incorporated in countless industrial and commercial applications. Because PFAS are nondegradable, they have been detected in all environmental media, indicating extensive global contamination. The unique physiochemical properties of PFAS and their complex interactions with environmental matrices create a great challenge for researchers when selecting site-specific sample matrices, sampling logistics, various analytical methods, and data interpretation. The widespread contamination and the potential toxicity of PFAS to human and environmental health have resulted in the proposed designation of two commonly used PFAS as hazardous substances, which may prompt new requirements for reporting, regulatory action, and site cleanup. For researchers involved in natural resource damage assessment efforts, understanding the multifaceted dynamics of the environmental fate and transport of PFAS will be essential for appropriate sample collections, analyses, and data interpretation. This guide aims to provide fundamental concepts and considerations involved with environmental sampling for PFAS during site assessments.\nPer- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS) are a large, diverse group of synthetic chemicals that have received considerable public attention because of the growing concerns regarding their impact on human and environmental health. This has resulted in PFAS being a priority subject of State and Federal strategic plans, new rules, and regulatory action in the United States and globally. Under the Comprehensive Environmental Response, Compensation, and Liability Act (CERCLA) of 1980 the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) is proposing to designate two commonly used PFAS, perfluorooctanesulfonic acid (PFOS) and perfluorooctanoic acid (PFOA), as hazardous substances. If these compounds are classified as hazardous substances, this may lead to spill reporting requirements, site cleanup, and remedial efforts (depending on site-specific conditions). Therefore, the purpose of this document is to introduce key PFAS concepts to personnel involved with Natural Resource Damage Assessment and Restoration (NRDAR) activities. This guide provides a brief overview of PFAS nomenclature and physiochemical properties (sec. 1), production, use, and regulatory status (sec. 1), environmental sources (sec. 1), environmental fate and transport (sec. 2), baseline considerations (sec. 3), response considerations (sec. 4), ephemeral data (sec. 5), restoration considerations (sec. 6), sampling techniques and collection preparation considerations (sec. 7), sampling design considerations (sec. 8), analytical methods (sec. 9), PFAS concentrations in environmental media (sec. 10), and a summary of key considerations (sec. 11). Table 1 provides the name and abbreviation for individual PFAS used throughout this document.\nNomenclature and Physicochemical Properties\nPFAS are a complex class of structurally diverse synthetic compounds that consist of different physical and chemical properties (Buck and others, 2011; Cousins and others, 2020). There are at least nine definitions of PFAS provided by several organizations that differ from being very broad (carbon + fluorine atoms) to highly descriptive (branched; Hammel and others, 2022); however, PFAS generally contain a tail or a carbon chain (C) of varying length (C3–14) and fluorination attached to a head containing a charged functional group (for example, oxygen, hydrogen, nitrogen, carboxylates, sulfonates; fig. 1). PFAS with a fully fluorinated carbon chain (backbone; where the hydrogen atoms are entirely replaced by fluorine atoms) are known as perfluoroalkyl substances, whereas PFAS with carbon chains that are partially fluorinated are known as polyfluoroalkyl substances (fig. 1).\nThe PFAS family has been estimated to include nearly 16,000 (with and without explicit chemical structures) chemical substances (EPA, 2023a). A few key publications discuss in depth the nomenclature and terminology of PFAS classifications (Buck and others, 2011; Cousins and others, 2020; Organisation for Economic Cooperation and Development, 2021; Wang and others, 2021); however, the Interstate Technology Regulatory Council (ITRC) has summarized the diverse variety of PFAS properties into a family tree consisting of two primary classes: polymers and nonpolymers (ITRC, 2021b). The nonpolymer class consists of two subclasses: the perfluoroalkyl and polyfluoroalkyl substances; furthermore, the group of perfluoroalkyl acids (PFAA)—such as perfluoroalkyl carboxylic acids (PFCA: such as PFOA) and perfluoroalkane sulfonic acids (PFSA; such as PFOS)—tend to be classified as either long-chain or short-chain compounds. The short-chain PFCA refers to those compounds with seven or fewer carbons of which six or less are perfluorinated, whereas their long-chain counterparts contain eight or more carbons of which seven or more are perfluorinated. Additionally, the short-chain PFSAs are those with five or fewer carbons that are perfluorinated, whereas the long-chain PFSAs are considered those with six or more carbons that are perfluorinated. Tables 1 and 2 summarize the nomenclature and physiochemical characteristics of the PFAS included in the widely accepted EPA and American Society for Testing and Materials (ASTM) methods further discussed in section 10.\nTable 1.\nList of names, abbreviations, class and group information for individual alkyl substances (PFAS) commonly included in monitoring efforts.[CASRN, Chemical Abstracts Service Registry Number]\nTable 2.\nAvailable partitioning coefficients for the individual per- and polyfluoroalkyl substance (PFAS) analytes included in commercially available laboratory .\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_14_v27\_codex_stdout.txt:1108:outputs\audits\v25\_codex_stdout.txt:1138:   21: A systematic review and meta-analysis of phase II/III trials confirmed that tirzepatide treatment resulted in a pooled mean HbA1c reduction of −1.94% and body weight reduction of −8.47 kg, with greater efficacy at higher doses.[20] Another meta-analysis reported similar incremental benefits: HbA1c was reduced by −0.20% with 10 mg and −0.30% with 15 mg versus 5 mg, and body weight was reduced by −2.64 kg with 10 mg and −4.26 kg with 15 mg versus 5 mg.[21] A Bayesian network meta-analysis ranked the 15 mg dose as having the highest probability (93.5%) of being the best option for HbA1c reduction and the highest probability (99.7%) for body weight reduction among active comparators.[3] The time to achieve glycemic targets also demonstrates a dose-response relationship; a pre-planned analysis of SURPASS-2 and SURPASS-3 found the median time to achieve HbA1c <7.0% was 8.1 weeks for all three tirzepatide doses, faster than with semaglutide 1 mg (12.0 weeks) or insulin degludec (12.1 weeks).[22] Regarding safety, gastrointestinal adverse events are the most common and show a dose-dependent increase in incidence.[23] Another analysis found the risk of adverse events was higher for the 10 mg (RR 1.20) and 15 mg (RR 1.26) doses compared to 5 mg.[21] Trial data corroborate this pattern; in SURPASS J-mono, the incidence of nausea was 12%, 20%, and 20% for the 5, 10, and 15 mg doses, respectively, versus 8% with dulaglutide 0.75 mg.[1] However, the incidence of serious adverse events and treatment discontinuation due to adverse events does not consistently show a clear dose-response trend across all trials.[23][21] A subgroup analysis of SURPASS-AP-Combo found the incidence of gastrointestinal adverse events was generally similar across patient subgroups defined by baseline characteristics like age, BMI, and HbA1c for each tirzepatide dose.[24][25]

.\outputs\codex_findings\full_audit_pass_15\codex_stderr.log:2771:-a---          2026-04-18  2:01 PM          20584 CLAUDE.md
.\outputs\codex_findings\full_audit_pass_15\codex_stderr.log:3758:outputs\campaigns\CAMP_20260310T073230_fb78bf\WEB_20260310T073230_5da0fc.json:891:      "content": "Abstract\nElectronic waste (e-waste) is a growing solid waste stream with largely undisclosed and poorly characterized fluorinated constituents. We evaluated per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS) leachability from four e-waste components (phone screens, phone plastics, capacitors, and Lithium-ion batteries) using a 30-day deionized water leaching test. PFAS were extracted by solid-phase extraction using weak anion exchange (WAX) cartridges and analyzed with a liquid chromatography triple-quadrupole mass spectrometer. In addition, the PFAS chemical profiles of e-waste components were characterized by non-targeted analysis. Leachable sums of detected PFAS (∑PFAS) were highest in phone screens (1739–1932 ng·kg−1) and phone plastics (1575–2197 ng·kg−1) and an order of magnitude lower in Lithium-ion batteries (148–158 ng·kg−1) and capacitors (147–243 ng·kg−1). Short-chain perfluoroalkyl acids (PFAAs) (e.g., PFBA, PFHxA) and legacy acids (e.g., PFOA, PFNA) were more prevalent in phone screens/plastics, whereas capacitors and batteries showed mixed sulfonate/carboxylate patterns (PFOS, PFHxS, and 6:2 FTS). Although capacitors and Lithium-ion batteries contained essential PFAS with high hazard potential at trace levels, phone screens and phone plastics pose a greater risk per mass due to higher ∑PFAS levels and larger volumes. Non-targeted analysis using Orbitrap Astral revealed CF2/CF2O homologous trends (confidence levels 2–3) with corroborating targeted findings. These findings highlight the need for PFAS-free alternatives, the disclosure of fluorinated additives, and stronger end-of-life management strategies to prevent PFAS releases from e-waste.\nKeywords: e-waste, PFAS, waste management, non-targeted analysis (NTA), partitioning behavior\n1. Introduction\nElectronic waste (also known as e-waste) is one of the fastest growing solid waste streams globally, driven by rapid technological advancements, increasing consumer demand, and short product lifespans [1]. Devices such as smartphones, laptops, and household electronics contain a complex array of components, including metals, polymers, ceramics, and adhesives [2]. Among these materials, increasing attention is being drawn to the presence of chemical additives, especially persistent and hazardous substances such as per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS) [3,4]. PFAS are synthetic organofluorine compounds used extensively in the manufacturing of electronic components due to their thermal stability, resistance to water, oil, and stains, and unique surface-active properties. In electronics, PFAS are commonly used as surfactants, flame retardants, dielectric fluids, lubricants, and polymer processing aids [5]. However, their resistance to environmental degradation and potential for bioaccumulation raise concerns about the release of PFAS from discarded electronic components into the environment [6].\nThe growing body of evidence indicates that PFAS are not only present in aqueous firefighting foams and industrial wastes but also embedded in consumer goods, including electronic devices such as Lithium-ion batteries, capacitors, phone screens, and plastic housings, either as functional additives or as processing residues [3,4,5,7]. Over time, in uncontrolled recycling or disposal, including open dumping, informal dismantling, or landfilling, these chemicals may be mobilized into surrounding soils and water systems through leaching. Laboratory-based leaching simulations, which mimic environmental conditions such as rainfall infiltration, acidic media, or landfill leachate, provide critical insights into the potential for PFAS to migrate from solid matrices into liquid phases [3,4,8,9,10,11,12,13]. These simulations are needed for understanding the risks posed by improperly managed e-waste in both developed and developing nations, where e-waste management infrastructure remains inadequate.\nTargeted PFAS analysis focuses on a select number of known PFAS (typically 20–40 compounds), which represent only a small fragment of the thousands of PFAS identified to date [14]. However, because the chemical profiles of most additives used in the manufacturing of electronic devices are not fully disclosed, the specific type of PFAS or precursors present in these products remain unknown. Non-targeted analysis (NTA) workflows allow for the discovery and screening of a much broader spectrum of fluorinated substances, including emerging and previously unreported compounds for complex matrices, such as e-waste, where proprietary chemical formulations and manufacturing variability make comprehensive identification challenging [15]. Using a non-targeted approach can resolve component-specific PFAS fingerprints and reveal a chemical diversity that is not completely captured by conventional targeted methods. When combined with leaching tests, these approaches enable the comprehensive characterization of PFAS levels and environmental mobility in solid waste matrices.\nDespite growing concern, research on PFAS in e-waste remains relatively limited, particularly compared with traditional contaminants such as heavy metals and brominated flame retardants [16]. Current regulatory frameworks and waste treatment protocols often overlook the presence of fluorinated compounds in electronics [17]. Moreover, there is limited knowledge of how different components, such as Lithium-ion batteries, capacitors, phone screens, and plastic casings, differ in their PFAS content, chemical profiles, and leaching behaviors. This knowledge gap hinders policymakers, recyclers, and environmental agencies in implementing effective control strategies or remediation technologies.\nWith the increased volume of electronic waste and the recognition of PFAS as contaminants of emerging concern, it is critical to understand the presence, levels, and fate of PFAS in electronic components. A few studies have reported PFAS in e-waste [16,18,19]. This limited data on the types of PFAS used and their leaching potential poses a significant risk to both the environment and public health. A previous study by our team identified and quantified 21 PFAS in leachates from e-waste components from computers, including cables, keyboards, e-boards, and monitors [3]. Thirty-day batch leaching tests showed that PFAS concentrations decreased in the following order, cables > keyboards > e-boards > monitors, while potential toxicity was highest in keyboards, followed by cables, e-boards, and monitors. The contribution of e-waste to PFAS release in the environment remains largely overlooked in global waste and water quality management due to limited and non-representative data and a lack of standardized monitoring procedures. This study addresses this gap by conducting leaching tests and the targeted and non-targeted PFAS screening of selected e-waste components to assess the potential risks of e-waste as a PFAS source.\n2. Results and Discussion\n2.1. PFAS Content in E-Waste Components\nA total of 16 PFAS were detected among the 40 targeted PFAS across all e-waste components; however, the distribution of individual concentrations and total PFAS concentrations differed markedly by the e-waste component. Table 1 presents the results expressed in ng·kg−1. The list of all 40 PFAS analyzed in this study is provided in Table S1. About 44% of the compounds were detected in all e-waste components, while 56% were detected in at least one component. The most prevalent compounds were 6:2 FTS, PFOA, PFOS, PFNA, PFDoA, and PFHpA, with detection frequencies of 75–100%. In terms of concentrations detected, PFBA, PFOA, PFNA, and PFHxA were the primary contributors to the total sum of PFAS (∑PFAS) across most components. The ∑PFAS in phone screens was the highest (1739–1932 ng·kg−1), followed by phone plastics (1575–2197 ng·kg−1), while Lithium-ion batteries and capacitors were an order of magnitude lower (148–158 and 147–243 ng·kg−1, respectively).\nTable 1.\n| PFAS | DF (%) | Phone Screen (ng·kg−1) |\nLithium-Ion Battery (ng·kg−1) |\nCapacitor (ng·kg−1) |\nPhone Plastics (ng·kg−1) |\n|---|---|---|---|---|---|\n| PFBA | 50.0 | 873 ± 108 | <0.0900 | <0.0900 | 426 ± 54.4 |\n| PF4OPeA | 25.0 | <0.0400 | <0.0400 | <0.0400 | 13.4 ± 1.58 |\n| PFPeA | 25.0 | <0.0400 | <0.0400 | <0.0400 | 165 ± 4.60 |\n| L-PFBS | 25.0 | <0.0200 | <0.0200 | <0.0200 | 621 ± 182 |\n| PFHxA | 50.0 | 112 ± 22.3 | <0.0200 | <0.0200 | 140 ± 3.44 |\n| GenX | 25.0 | 2.88 ± 4.08 | <0.0400 | <0.0400 | 0.634 ± 0.896 |\n| PFHxS | 63.0 | 10.7 ± 15.2 | 13.3 ± 18.8 | 8.00 ± 0.472 | 2.53 ± 3.58 |\n| PFHpA | 75.0 | 21.2 ± 0.480 | <0.0200 | 5.32 ± 0.0800 | 27.8 ± 0.615 |\n| 6-2 FTS | 100 | 30.4 ± 0.759 | 8.53 ± 0.476 | 15.5 ± 0.520 | 18.7 ± 1.29 |\n| PFHpS | 50.0 | 12.5 ± 0.139 | <0.0600 | 6.57 ± 0.585 | <0.0600 |\n| PFOA | 88.0 | 560 ± 31.7 | 33.3 ± 4.29 | 51.6 ± 12.4 | 178 ± 252 |\n| PFOS | 88.0 | 74.2 ± 3.23 | 68.0 ± 12.3 | 71.7 ± 3.60 | 17.7 ± 25.1 |\n| PFNA | 88.0 | 128 ± 7.76 | 18.4 ± 8.23 | 34.7 ± 49.0 | 206 ± 9.20 |\n| 8-2 FTS | 50.0 | 6.30 ± 8.91 | 7.70 ± 10.9 | <0.0200 | 10.5 ± 5.96 |\n| N-MeFOSAA | 63.0 | <0.0600 | 2.37 ± 0.938 | 0.676 ± 0.324 | 0.614 ± 0.868 |\n| PFDoA | 75.0 | 4.88 ± 6.89 | 1.44 ± 0.190 | 0.228 ± 0.322 | 43.5 ± 20.2 |\n| ∑PFAS | - | 1739–1932 | 148–158 | 147–243 | 1575–2197 |\nValues are reported as mean ± SD for n = 2 independent composite replicates, each comprising ≥4 items. DF = detection frequency. SD = standard deviation.\nA one-way ANOVA showed that the e-waste components significantly influenced total PFAS concentrations (F3,4 = 35.01, p = 0.0025), indicating substantial differences in PFAS burdens among the four components examined. Tukey’s HSD post hoc tests show that mobile phone screens and phone plastics had significantly higher total PFAS concentrations than Lithium-ion batteries and capacitors (all p < 0.01). No significant differences were observed between mobile phone screens and phone plastics or between Lithium-ion batteries and capacitors (p > 0.99). Each analytical replicate repre"
.\outputs\codex_findings\full_audit_pass_15\codex_stderr.log:4085:outputs\polaris_graph\PG_LB_FS_02_materials.json:305:      "content": "Title: 1320380\n\nURL Source: https://www.osti.gov/servlets/purl/1320380\n\nNumber of Pages: 20\n\nMarkdown Content:\nAdhesion and debonding kinetics of photovoltaic  encapsulation \n\n# in moist environments \n\n## Fernand o D. Novoa 1, David C. Miller 2 and Reinhold H. Dauskardt 1*  \n\n> 1Department of Materials Science and Engineering, Stanford University, Stanford, CA 94305 -2205, USA\n> 2Nati onal Center for Photovoltaics, NREL, Golden, CO, USA\n\n# ABSTRACT \n\nDebonding of phot ovoltaic (PV) encapsulation in moist environments is frequently reported but presently \n\nnot well understood or quanti fi ed. Temperature cycling, moisture, and mechanical loads oft en cause loss of \n\nencapsulation adhesion and  interfacial debonding, initially fa cilitating back -re fl ectance and reduced \n\nelectrical current, but ultimately leading to internal  corrosion and loss of module functionality. To \n\ninvestigate the effects of temperat ure ( T) and relative humidity (RH) on the  kinetics of encapsulation \n\ndebonding,  we developed a mechanics -based technique to measure encapsulation debond energy  and \n\ndebond growth rates in a chamber of controlled environment. The debond energy decreased from  2.15 to \n\n1.75 kJ m 2 in  poly(ethylene -co -vinyl acetate) (EVA) and from 0.67 to 0. 52 kJ m 2 in polyvinyl butyral when  T\n\nincreased from 25 to 50° C and 20 to 40° C, respectively. The debond growth rates of EVA increased up to \n\n1000 -fold with small increases of  T (10° C)  and RH (15%). To elucidate the mechanisms of environmental \n\ndebonding, we  developed a fracture -kinetics model, where  the viscoelastic relaxation processes at the \n\ndebonding -tip are used to predict debond growth. The model and techniques  constitute the  fundamental \n\nbasis for developing accelerated aging tests and long -term reliabil ity predictions for PV  encapsulation.  \n\n> KEYWORDS encapsulation debonding; interfacial adhesion; moisture; delamination; durability\n\n# 1.  INTRODUCTION  Photovoltaic (PV) encapsulatio n is arguably the most important structural \n\ncomponent of PV modules. It holds the  module together and protects it from aggressive  fi eld  environments \n\nthat include moisture, external mechanical  loads, and temperature cycles[1,2] . Guaranteeing the  long -term \n\n(up to 25 years) interfacial stability of PV encapsulation is currently a remarkable challenge; preventing \n\ninterfacial debonding of similar  fi lm technologies in othe  industries has so far proven dif fi cult and costly[3]. \n\nUpon  encapsulation debonding, additio nal optical interfaces are  generated within the module, resulting in \n\nlocal backre fl ection and reduced electrical current. Following  debonding, several corrosion processes of the \n\nmodule  components can accelerate dramatically b ecause of direct  contact with t he environment[4,5]. PV \n\nencapsulation  debonding can therefore be a crucial limiting factor for  the yield and reliability of PV modules, \n\neventually risking  loss of function[6,7]. Although the effect of hot and humid  fi eld e nvironments on \n\nencapsulation degra dation is often  reported[4,6,8], the fundamental kinetic processes that  lead to \n\ndebonding have not yet been characterized . Debonding in similar structures, however, has been shown  to \n\ndepend strongly on moisture[9,10], temp erature[11,12],  mechanical stress[ 13], and their synergistic \n\ninteractions  [14]. The effects of these variables on PV encapsulation  debonding are important not only to \n\nassess long -term  encapsulation reliability, which may limit module lifetime, but also to  develop tests and \n\nprotocols to imp rove  encapsulation formulation(s) and manufacturing (surfacepreparation) procedures. \n\nEncapsulation debonding occurs when the strain energy release rate, G (J m 2), or debond driving force,  is \n\nequal to the critical debond  energy (adhesion) at the encapsulat ion interface, G c. However, debonding  in the \n\npresence of active environments can occur at debond -driving forces below G c, because of the  effects of \n\nmoisture and temperature on the kinetic processes that occur at the debonding -tip, such as  viscoelastic rela xation, chemical reactions, and water transport. Encapsulation adhesion has been  previously studied \n\nusing peeling -based techniques includ ing the 180°[15 –17], 90°“T” [1,18,19], and  load -controlled 45°peel \n\ntests [20]. These techniques presently dominate th e study of encapsulation  and backsheet adhesion, but the \n\nresulting peel -strength is not an interfacial property and cannot be  used to com pare between dissimilar \n\nmaterials. Peel -strength results include macroscopic plastic  deformation of the encapsulation m aterial and \n\nthe applied handle layer (tape used to perform the  peel -test), which are difficult to quantify[21 –23], \n\nunrelated to the adhes ive mechanism, and irrelevant  to infield debonding. Practically, deviation of peel angle \n\nfrom its nominal value as well  as cavitation  (because of the large separation angle) also reduce \n\ncomparability between materials. More importantly,  debonding of viscoe lastic films is strongly affected by \n\nthe debonding -tip deformation processes  [9,10,24], which are not well represented  by the various peeling \n\ntechniques. \n\nOther mechanical methods  that have been applied to study PV encapsulation attachment interfaces includ e\n\nthe overlap shear  test[2,25], rotational torque[26], compressive shear test[27], wedge test[28 –30], and the \n\nblister  test  [31,32]. The former methods examine the strength of the attachment layer rather than interfacial \n\nadhesion to the relevant substrate.  The overlap shear test has long been known to be limited by the \n\nmechanical compliance (displacement per unit load) of  the specimen handles[33 –35]. The compressive \n\nshear test or double -lap shear[33] specimen geometries can be used to avoid this limitation.  While  insight \n\nmay be gained by analogy using the former methods, the results may not be directly related to  the \n\nfundam ental mechanic and kinetic processes of debonding. The wedge and blister methods do  instead make \n\nuse of fundamental fracture mechanics pri nciples, but they have not yet been  demonstrated using PV \n\nmaterials. The wedge test, however, may not be capable of de termining the  initial (unaged) G c in strongly \n\nadhered systems, like PV encapsulation. \n\nThe effect of simulated  environments on encapsulatio n adhesion has been mostly studied by replicating \n\ntests and sequences  from the module qualification protocols[36,37].  The “UV exposure,” “thermal cycling,” \n\nand “humidity  freeze” tests simulate a brief field exposure (months to a few years at best), whereas  the \n\nconditions  applied in “damp heat” test greatly exceed those encountered in the application [38]. The \n\nqualificati on  tests at best query infant mortality failure modes and do not of themselves investigate the rate \n\nof  degradation. The currently available  aging treatments, as well as the available adhesion tests, provide \n\nlimited insight on the kinetic processes of debon ding and their dependence on mechanical stress,  moisture \n\nand T. For example, softening of the encapsulant (lower E) can occur near the tran sition or  melting \n\ntemperatures, which can significantly reduce encapsulation adhesion and accelerate debonding. \n\nKinet ics -based testing protocols are therefore urgently needed not only to assess encapsulation  reliability \n\nand durability in field environments  but also to define objectives for encapsulation  formulation and \n\nmanufacturing (surface preparation) procedures. \n\nIn  this study, we develop a quantitative characterization  technique to measure the effects of mechanical \n\nstress,  T, and moisture on the debond energy and debond kinetics  of two encapsulation materials used in PV \n\nmodules: poly  (ethylene -co -vinyl acetate) (EVA)  and polyvinyl butyral  (PVB). To measure debond energy, we \n\nfabricated single  cantilever beam debond (SCB) specimens (Section 3.1)  and modi fi ed a well -known SCB test \n\nmetrology (Sections  3.2 and 3.3), where debonding is studied as the propagation  of an inter facial crack[39]. \n\nTo en sure debonding of the  glass/EVA interface, we describe a novel temperature gradient method. To \n\nmeasure encapsulation debond growth rate  (Section 3.4), d a/ dt, we applied a load -relaxation technique  to \n\nthe SCB debond specimens in an en vironment of controlled  temperature,  T, and relative humidity, RH. The \n\ntechnique allowed debond growth rates, d a/ dt, to be accurately  quanti fi ed (~10 4 m s 1 to 10 8) in terms of the \n\ndebonddriving force,  G, and the viscoelastic relaxation processes  at the deb onding -tip that depend  on temperature ( T) and relative humidity (RH). The synergistic effect of UV -light is not  examined in this study. \n\nTensile specimens were also fabricated (Section 3.5) to quantify the effect of temperature on  encapsulation \n\nstiffness, w hich we used to rationa lize the  debond -growth data. Debonding kinetics was modeled using \n\nviscoelastic fracture -kinetics (Section 4), including the softening effect of T and RH on the encapsulation \n\nmodulus.  The model provides a more fundamental understandin g of  the effects of the  environment on \n\nencapsulation debonding.  The techniques and model can be used to develop accelerated testing protocols, \n\nevaluate the adoption of new encapsulations, and make long -term reliability predictions. \n\n# 2. MATERIALS AND METHODS \n\n2.1. Encapsulation  SCB debond specimens \n\nIn order to fabricate representative encapsulation specimens, a 0.45 -mm layer of PV encapsulation (EVA or \n\nPVB) was laminated between a 5.5 -mm -thick glass substrate (low iron -containing, soda -lime  fl oat glass, as \n\nin  the  PV industry)  and a 3.17 -mm -thick titanium plate (6 Al -4 V, Grade -5), as shown in Figure 1a. Prior to \n\nlamination,  the surfaces of the Ti plate and the glass (Sn -de fi cient side)  were carefully cleaned, including \n\nbuf fi ng with pumice  powder, washing with a n aqueous detergent (Billco, Billco  Manufacturing Inc.), rinsing \n\nwith deionized water, and rinsing with isopropyl alcohol. The lamination step was performed in a \n\ncommercial instrument (Model LM -404,  Astropower Inc.) at 145° C for 8 min  under applied pressur e of 1 \n\natm. During lamination, the specimen components  were  fi xed relative to one another using Kapton tape and \n\nwere surrounded with a glass frame to improve thickness  uniformity at the edges. Two parallel cuts (12 -mm \n\napart)  were made through the full thic kness of the Ti plate and the  encapsulation layer with a high -speed \n\ntungsten carbide saw  and a razor blade, respectively (Figure 1b) .\n\nThe elastic beam material (Ti here) was chosen to  maximize elastic cantilever de fl ection  Δ y (m), as given by \n\nwhere  l, h,σy and  εy are the length, thickness, yield stress,  and yield strain of the beam, respectively, and  Gc is \n\nthe encapsulation debond energy, which is unknown before the  debond measurement, but is independent of \n\nbeam material . To maximize Δ y, we calculated the product  σyεy2 for several  candidate beam materials (in \n\nMPa), including steel (0.023),  Al (0.019), poly(methyl methacrylate) (0.039) and Ti  (0.047). We used Ti \n\nbeams in this study but some polymer  beams, such as poly(methy l methacrylate), can be sometimes easier \n\nto bond to the organic PV encapsulation[24]. \n\n2.2. SCB debond test metrology \n\nThe SCB specimen described in Section 3.1 was  fi xe d to a  rigid testing table with screw clamps. A loading tab \n\nwith a  ruby bearing was bonde d to one end of the Ti beam, and  the tab was then connected to an adhesion \n\ntest system  (Delaminator, DTS, Menlo Park, CA) consisting of a linear  actuator (displacem ent -controlled, 0.1 \n\nμm resolution) in  series with a high -resolution mechanical load cell  fi xed to  a high -stiffness frame. The \n\ndisplacement of the linear actuator and the mechanical load were monitored every 0.1 s. \n\nThe actuator was retracted (developing  a tensile force,  P) to  initiate encapsulation debonding, until the \n\ndebond  length (distance from the loading tab to the debondingtip) was ~15 mm. The Ti beam in this \n\ncon fi guration can  be treated as a single cantilever beam  fi xed at the  debonding -tip [39]. T he mechanical \n\ncomplianc e of the  SCB specimen (40 –60  μm/N) was much larger than that  of the testing system (~0.5 \n\nμm/N), which allowed for  accurate debond energy measurements. A minimum beam  thickness (~3 mm here) \n\nwas required to avoid plastic  deformation of  the beam due to bending stress during the  debonding \n\nexperiment. \n\nThe SCB specimen was mechanically loaded at a  constant displacement rate to propagate debonding for a few additional millimeters and then immediately unloaded  to prevent further debonding.  The \n\nloading -unloading  process was repeated for several  cycles at increasing  debond -lengths, adjusting the \n\nactuator displacement rate  to keep the local deformation rate (at the debonding -tip)  constant (refer to \n\nSection 3.5). At each debonding cycle,  the mechanical compliance (displacement per unit load)  of the  SCB \n\nspecimen,  C, and the mechanical load at the  onset of debonding,  Pc (where the load -displacement curve \n\nbecomes non -linear) were recorded. \n\nThe debond driving -force,  G (J m -2), can be than ca lculated with the following expression [40]: \n\nwhere  b is the w idth of the Ti beam and  a is the debondlength. The encapsulation debond energy,  Gc, is then \n\nde fi ned as the value of  G at the onset of debonding, where  P = P c.\n\nThe value of  C for the SCB s pecimen is also a function  of the encapsulation stiffness, which dep ends on  T and \n\nthe  mechanical loading -rate [41]. Using the DTS system  described in Section 3.3, the value of  C (m/N) was \n\nmeasured at selected values of  a at 20 and 50° C in a chamber  of cont rolled RH (38%). The compliance data \n\nwere then  fi tted to the complia nce equation for a cantilever beam with  elastic foundation [42]: \n\nwhere  E, h, and  G are the Young ’s modulus, thickness, and  shear modulus of the Ti beam; and  B is an \n\nencapsulationdependent parameter given by \n\nwhere  t, ν, and  EE are the thickness, Poisson ’s ratio, and  modulus of the encapsulation, respectively. In the \n\ncase of  very large  a or very thin elastic beams ( h/a ≪ 1),  Eqn 3  takes the form of the compliance equation for \n\nthe elastic  beam in cantilever without encap sulation [43] present,  where  C = 4 a3/( Ebh 3). \n\nMaximizing elastic beam de fl ection (thinnest beam)  reduces the contribution of the encapsulation layer to \n\nthe  overall SCB compliance, improves the sensitivity of the  debond experiment, and reduces the size of th e\n\ndamage  zone ahead of the debond -tip. We therefore selected the  smallest possible value of  h (3.17 mm here) \n\nfor the Ti  beam —corresponding to a maximum bend ing stress of  ~70% the yield strength of Ti. \n\n2.3. SCB debond test displacement rate \n\nThe debond -tip  displacement rate,  δ_ , which characterizes  the deformation rates at the debond -tip, was \n\nkept constant  at every debonding cycle to minimize rate -effects in the  debond energy measurement. To keep \n\nδ_ constant, we  derived an equation for the required actuator  displacement  rate ( Δ_ ) as a function of  a as \n\nfollows.  The debond -tip opening displacement, δ, is given by where  ε y is the yield strain of the encapsulation. (At  debonding,  δ = δc and  G = Gc.) \n\nSubstituting Eqns 2 and 3 into the time -derivative of  Eqn 5,  and neglecting second -order and higher -order \n\nterms, the actuator displacement rate  Δ_ (m/s) required  to keep  δ_ constant (at debonding) at increasing \n\ndebondlengths can be approximated as \n\nwhere  Δ_ 0 is the reference actuator displacement rate (at a  refer ence debond -length  a0). To illustrate the use \n\nof this  equation, we calculated the value of  δ_ (at debonding) as a  function of  a, using Eqns 2, 3, and 5 with a \n\nrepresentative  SCB specimen (Figure 2). The values of  δ_ and  a were normalized to the initial val ues,  δ0̇ and \n\na0 = 15  mm, respectively. The value of  δ_ decreases rapidly with  a when the  SCB is loaded at a constant \n\ndisplacement rate ( Δ_ 0) but remains approximately constant ( δ_=δ_ 0≈1) when loaded at the  corrected \n\ndisplacement rate ( Δ_ ) given by Eqn 6 .\n\nIn thinner and st iffer encapsulation layers, where  tE / EE ≪ h, or when the debond experiment starts at large \n\ninitial debond -lengths ( h0.75 /a0 ≪ 1), Eqn 6 reduces to \n\nwhich has been previously used to determine the actuator  displacement rates in debond e xperiments with \n\nthinner  and stiffer  fi lms [10,13] \n\n2.4. Debond growth rate kinetics \n\nA detailed explanation of the load -relaxation method for  debond growth measurement  is provided \n\nelsewhere  [12,39]; a brief summary is given here. To measure the  encapsulation  debond growth rate, d a/ dt,\n\nas a function of  G, the SCB specimens were mechanically loaded in the  DTS adhesion test system (Section 3.2) \n\nup to a value of  G close to t he encapsulation debond energy. The load  relaxation was then recorded as a \n\nfunction of tim e for about  24 h at a  fi xed actuator displacement. Post -experiment  analysis of the \n\nload -relaxation data determined the debond  growth rates, d a/ dt, over the range of ~ 10 8 to ~10 4 m s 1 as a \n\nfunction of  G. The experiments were performed on new  encapsulation s pecimens in an environmental \n\nchamber at  selected values of RH (10, 25, 40, 55, and 70%) and  T (20, 30, 40, and 50° C). The load cell and \n\nactuator were  allowed to equil ibrate in the chamber environment for  6–8 h before each experiment. \n\nThe debond path in the  debond growth experiments  shifted occasionally from the EVA/glass to the EVA/Ti \n\ninterface (and vice versa), which limited the yield of the  debond growth experiments described in the \n\npreceding paragraphs. In a separate set of experiments, a temperature gra dient  was established on th e EVA layer, which successfully  stabilized the debond path at the warmer EVA/glass interface. To establish the  T\n\ngradient, the glass side of the SCB  specimen was heated on a  “hot -plate ” at a  fi xed temperature  (48, 53, or \n\n58° C) an d the Ti side was expose d to the colder  air of an environmental chamber (20, 24, or 30° C). The \n\nheat -fl ow required to maintain this temperature gradient  (4.1 kJm 2) was ~4 times that of solar illumination \n\n(1 kJm 2) as calculated with a one -dimension Fourier h eat  fl ow model using the rmal conductivities of 22, 1, \n\nand  0.23W m 1 K1 for Ti, glass, and EVA [44], respectively.  The purpose of this experiment was not to emulate \n\nfi eldconditions but to investigate the effect of a temperature gradient on stabilizing the de bonding path to \n\nthe EVA/ glass interface. The temperatures at the glass/EVA (28.4, 32.5, and  38° C) and EVA/Ti (20.3, 24.4, \n\nand 30.3° C) were also  calculated with this model. The surface of the hotplate and  the sides of the SCB \n\nspecimen were covered with a 5 -mm  sheet of polystyrene  foam to minimize heat loss. The hot  plate, SCB \n\nspecimen, and the delaminator system were  allowed to stabilize in the environmental chamber for one  hour \n\nto establish the  T gradient across the glass/EVA/Ti  structure. Once the gradient  of ~8° C was established , the \n\ndebond growth experiment described in the preceding paragraph was conducted. \n\n2.5. Tensile specimens \n\nThe SCB mechanical compliance and optimal SCB  testing displacement rate (employed to characterize d a/ dt\n\nand  G c) depend on the  value of the encapsulation modulus  (E)  as described in Sections 3.2 and 3.3. In order \n\nto fabricate  tensile experiments for module measurement, a 0.45 -mm  layer of EVA was laminated between \n\ntwo glass substrates.  Several 3.5 -mm square specimen s were then cut  from the  laminated structure with a \n\nhigh -speed precision saw to make  glass/EVA/glass tensile specimens (3.5 × 3.5 × 10.5 mm). To  measure the \n\nvalue of  E of the EVA layer, a loading tab was  attached to each glass substrate and a monotonic loa d was \n\napplied f rom 0.5 to +0.5MPa in an environmental chamber of controlled relative humidity (38%). The \n\nmodulus  was measured at selected values of  T in recently laminated  specimens (10 days) and in specimens \n\naged for 1 year in a  laboratory environment (23 °C,  fl uorescent  indoor illumination of ~200 lux, and RH \n\nranging from 25 to 45%). The  cross -section area of the tensile specimen corresponded to a  mixed state of \n\nplane -stress (edges) and plane -strain (center)  in the EVA layer. \n\n# 3. RESULTS AND DISCUSSION \n\n3.1. Environmental  sensitivity of EVA  modulus \n\nThe elastic modulus of the EVA encapsulation ( E) as a  function of testing temperature,  T, is shown in Figure \n\n3 for recently laminated (10 days) and laboratory -aged  (1 year) specimens. Aging was conducted at 2 3° C, \n\nunder  fl uorescen t indoor illumination of ~200 lux, and 25 to  45% RH. The storage modulus of EVA,  EDMA ,\n\nmeasured  with dynamic mechanical analysis (DMA) in a previous  experiment by one of the authors [45], is \n\nalso included.  The value of  E and  EDMA  decreased exponentially wi th  T in the entropy -elastic temperature \n\nrange (30 –60° C) below  the melting temperature (65° C),  Tm. The value of  E increased ~10 -fold with aging, \n\nwhich  was attributed to  thermal and possible photochemical cross -linking of EVA  [4] although the aged \n\nspecimens  were exposed only to  indoor illumination ( fl uorescent). To explain, recently  laminated EVA \n\nencapsulation may still contain as much  as 40% of the init ial peroxide used to facilitate cross -linking  [47,48]. \n\nIn  fi elded PV modules, the combination of residual  peroxide and UV radiation explains the cross -linking of \n\nEVA to up to ~90% gel content. \n\nThe Arrhenius equation was  fi tted to the inverse of the  meas ured moduli ( E1 and  EDMA 1) as shown in Figure \n\n3 in dotted lines ( T < Tm), with an activation energy of ~37  kJ  mol 1 in the new specimens and 40kJ mol 1 in \n\nthe aged  specimens, respectively. The similarity between the  activation energies suggests that aging does \n\nnot affect signi fi cantly the temperature -dependence of the compliance  over the one year age. Similarly, the \n\nmelting temperature,  Tm (65° C) [41] did not change signi fi cantly. At  T > Tm, where the polymer is in th e melt \n\nstate, the values of  EDMA  (1.1 MPa) and  E (0.7 and 4 MPa) were independent of  T.3.2. Mechanical compliance of SCB debond  specimen \n\nThe mechanical  compliance,  C, of the SCB specimen as a  function of debond length ( a) is shown in Figure 4 \n\nfor  EVA (circle data points) and PVB (square data points)  specimens, at two selected values of  T. The \n\ncompliance of  the bare Ti cantilever beam is also shown for re ference as  a solid line. The value of  C increased \n\nwith  a in the SC B specimens and in the bare Ti beam. The specimen with  the lowest encapsulation storage \n\nmodulus, PVB ( G′~0.4  to 40 MPa) [49], exhibited the largest SCB compliance,  followed by the specimen with \n\nEVA (G ′~20 to  1000 MPa) [41], followed by the bare Ti beam. The  value of  C increased with  T as a \n\nconsequence of the  temperature -dependence of the encapsulation modul us;  this dependence was stronger \n\nin the more compliant  encapsulation (PVB) \n\nTo obtain an analytical exp ression of  C as a function of  a, we  fi tted Eqn 3 to the compliance data of the SCB \n\nspecimens with EVA (dashed -line) and PVB (dotted -line),  as shown in Fig ure 4. The  fi tted equations were \n\nthen used  to calculate the encapsulation debond energy (Eqn 2) as  des cribed in Section 3.2. \n\n3.3. Encapsulation debond energy \n\nThe measured debond energy of the EVA/glass interface,  G c, decreased from 2.15 to 1.8 kJ m 2 in th e 25 to \n\n50° C temperature range (Figure 5). At temperatures close to  Tm (65° C), the value of  Gc decreased six -fold to \n\n0.3 kJ m 2. The value of  G c remained constant in the melt state at  any  T, similar to the modulus in Figure 3. \n\nThe debond energy of the PVB/ Ti interface, shown in  Figure 5 (circular data points), also decreased with  T\n\nin  the 20 to 40° C temperature range. The values of  Gc for  PVB/Ti (0.67 to 0.52 kJ m 2) were much lower than \n\nthose  of EVA/glass in the same temperature range, which can be  attribut ed to the lower yield stress of PVB \n\nand to the nature  of the attachment at the  interface. EVA is typically bonded  to glass covalently, as enabled \n\nthrough a silane chemistry  (primer) typically present in the EVA formulation[27]. In  contrast, PVB is more \n\nwea kly bonded via hydrogen  bonding [27]. \n\nTo better understand the effect of te mperature on  debond energy, Eqn 5 can be solved for  Gc (J m 2) [9]: \n\nwhere  E(t, T) is the temperature -dependent modulus of the  material at the debonding -tip. \n\nIn the EVA/glass interf ace, the extent of plastic deformation during debonding is determined  "
.\outputs\codex_findings\full_audit_pass_15\codex_stderr.log:4107:outputs\polaris_graph\baseline_v2_materials\PG_LB_FS_02_materials.json:305:      "content": "Title: 1320380\n\nURL Source: https://www.osti.gov/servlets/purl/1320380\n\nNumber of Pages: 20\n\nMarkdown Content:\nAdhesion and debonding kinetics of photovoltaic  encapsulation \n\n# in moist environments \n\n## Fernand o D. Novoa 1, David C. Miller 2 and Reinhold H. Dauskardt 1*  \n\n> 1Department of Materials Science and Engineering, Stanford University, Stanford, CA 94305 -2205, USA\n> 2Nati onal Center for Photovoltaics, NREL, Golden, CO, USA\n\n# ABSTRACT \n\nDebonding of phot ovoltaic (PV) encapsulation in moist environments is frequently reported but presently \n\nnot well understood or quanti fi ed. Temperature cycling, moisture, and mechanical loads oft en cause loss of \n\nencapsulation adhesion and  interfacial debonding, initially fa cilitating back -re fl ectance and reduced \n\nelectrical current, but ultimately leading to internal  corrosion and loss of module functionality. To \n\ninvestigate the effects of temperat ure ( T) and relative humidity (RH) on the  kinetics of encapsulation \n\ndebonding,  we developed a mechanics -based technique to measure encapsulation debond energy  and \n\ndebond growth rates in a chamber of controlled environment. The debond energy decreased from  2.15 to \n\n1.75 kJ m 2 in  poly(ethylene -co -vinyl acetate) (EVA) and from 0.67 to 0. 52 kJ m 2 in polyvinyl butyral when  T\n\nincreased from 25 to 50° C and 20 to 40° C, respectively. The debond growth rates of EVA increased up to \n\n1000 -fold with small increases of  T (10° C)  and RH (15%). To elucidate the mechanisms of environmental \n\ndebonding, we  developed a fracture -kinetics model, where  the viscoelastic relaxation processes at the \n\ndebonding -tip are used to predict debond growth. The model and techniques  constitute the  fundamental \n\nbasis for developing accelerated aging tests and long -term reliabil ity predictions for PV  encapsulation.  \n\n> KEYWORDS encapsulation debonding; interfacial adhesion; moisture; delamination; durability\n\n# 1.  INTRODUCTION  Photovoltaic (PV) encapsulatio n is arguably the most important structural \n\ncomponent of PV modules. It holds the  module together and protects it from aggressive  fi eld  environments \n\nthat include moisture, external mechanical  loads, and temperature cycles[1,2] . Guaranteeing the  long -term \n\n(up to 25 years) interfacial stability of PV encapsulation is currently a remarkable challenge; preventing \n\ninterfacial debonding of similar  fi lm technologies in othe  industries has so far proven dif fi cult and costly[3]. \n\nUpon  encapsulation debonding, additio nal optical interfaces are  generated within the module, resulting in \n\nlocal backre fl ection and reduced electrical current. Following  debonding, several corrosion processes of the \n\nmodule  components can accelerate dramatically b ecause of direct  contact with t he environment[4,5]. PV \n\nencapsulation  debonding can therefore be a crucial limiting factor for  the yield and reliability of PV modules, \n\neventually risking  loss of function[6,7]. Although the effect of hot and humid  fi eld e nvironments on \n\nencapsulation degra dation is often  reported[4,6,8], the fundamental kinetic processes that  lead to \n\ndebonding have not yet been characterized . Debonding in similar structures, however, has been shown  to \n\ndepend strongly on moisture[9,10], temp erature[11,12],  mechanical stress[ 13], and their synergistic \n\ninteractions  [14]. The effects of these variables on PV encapsulation  debonding are important not only to \n\nassess long -term  encapsulation reliability, which may limit module lifetime, but also to  develop tests and \n\nprotocols to imp rove  encapsulation formulation(s) and manufacturing (surfacepreparation) procedures. \n\nEncapsulation debonding occurs when the strain energy release rate, G (J m 2), or debond driving force,  is \n\nequal to the critical debond  energy (adhesion) at the encapsulat ion interface, G c. However, debonding  in the \n\npresence of active environments can occur at debond -driving forces below G c, because of the  effects of \n\nmoisture and temperature on the kinetic processes that occur at the debonding -tip, such as  viscoelastic rela xation, chemical reactions, and water transport. Encapsulation adhesion has been  previously studied \n\nusing peeling -based techniques includ ing the 180°[15 –17], 90°“T” [1,18,19], and  load -controlled 45°peel \n\ntests [20]. These techniques presently dominate th e study of encapsulation  and backsheet adhesion, but the \n\nresulting peel -strength is not an interfacial property and cannot be  used to com pare between dissimilar \n\nmaterials. Peel -strength results include macroscopic plastic  deformation of the encapsulation m aterial and \n\nthe applied handle layer (tape used to perform the  peel -test), which are difficult to quantify[21 –23], \n\nunrelated to the adhes ive mechanism, and irrelevant  to infield debonding. Practically, deviation of peel angle \n\nfrom its nominal value as well  as cavitation  (because of the large separation angle) also reduce \n\ncomparability between materials. More importantly,  debonding of viscoe lastic films is strongly affected by \n\nthe debonding -tip deformation processes  [9,10,24], which are not well represented  by the various peeling \n\ntechniques. \n\nOther mechanical methods  that have been applied to study PV encapsulation attachment interfaces includ e\n\nthe overlap shear  test[2,25], rotational torque[26], compressive shear test[27], wedge test[28 –30], and the \n\nblister  test  [31,32]. The former methods examine the strength of the attachment layer rather than interfacial \n\nadhesion to the relevant substrate.  The overlap shear test has long been known to be limited by the \n\nmechanical compliance (displacement per unit load) of  the specimen handles[33 –35]. The compressive \n\nshear test or double -lap shear[33] specimen geometries can be used to avoid this limitation.  While  insight \n\nmay be gained by analogy using the former methods, the results may not be directly related to  the \n\nfundam ental mechanic and kinetic processes of debonding. The wedge and blister methods do  instead make \n\nuse of fundamental fracture mechanics pri nciples, but they have not yet been  demonstrated using PV \n\nmaterials. The wedge test, however, may not be capable of de termining the  initial (unaged) G c in strongly \n\nadhered systems, like PV encapsulation. \n\nThe effect of simulated  environments on encapsulatio n adhesion has been mostly studied by replicating \n\ntests and sequences  from the module qualification protocols[36,37].  The “UV exposure,” “thermal cycling,” \n\nand “humidity  freeze” tests simulate a brief field exposure (months to a few years at best), whereas  the \n\nconditions  applied in “damp heat” test greatly exceed those encountered in the application [38]. The \n\nqualificati on  tests at best query infant mortality failure modes and do not of themselves investigate the rate \n\nof  degradation. The currently available  aging treatments, as well as the available adhesion tests, provide \n\nlimited insight on the kinetic processes of debon ding and their dependence on mechanical stress,  moisture \n\nand T. For example, softening of the encapsulant (lower E) can occur near the tran sition or  melting \n\ntemperatures, which can significantly reduce encapsulation adhesion and accelerate debonding. \n\nKinet ics -based testing protocols are therefore urgently needed not only to assess encapsulation  reliability \n\nand durability in field environments  but also to define objectives for encapsulation  formulation and \n\nmanufacturing (surface preparation) procedures. \n\nIn  this study, we develop a quantitative characterization  technique to measure the effects of mechanical \n\nstress,  T, and moisture on the debond energy and debond kinetics  of two encapsulation materials used in PV \n\nmodules: poly  (ethylene -co -vinyl acetate) (EVA)  and polyvinyl butyral  (PVB). To measure debond energy, we \n\nfabricated single  cantilever beam debond (SCB) specimens (Section 3.1)  and modi fi ed a well -known SCB test \n\nmetrology (Sections  3.2 and 3.3), where debonding is studied as the propagation  of an inter facial crack[39]. \n\nTo en sure debonding of the  glass/EVA interface, we describe a novel temperature gradient method. To \n\nmeasure encapsulation debond growth rate  (Section 3.4), d a/ dt, we applied a load -relaxation technique  to \n\nthe SCB debond specimens in an en vironment of controlled  temperature,  T, and relative humidity, RH. The \n\ntechnique allowed debond growth rates, d a/ dt, to be accurately  quanti fi ed (~10 4 m s 1 to 10 8) in terms of the \n\ndebonddriving force,  G, and the viscoelastic relaxation processes  at the deb onding -tip that depend  on temperature ( T) and relative humidity (RH). The synergistic effect of UV -light is not  examined in this study. \n\nTensile specimens were also fabricated (Section 3.5) to quantify the effect of temperature on  encapsulation \n\nstiffness, w hich we used to rationa lize the  debond -growth data. Debonding kinetics was modeled using \n\nviscoelastic fracture -kinetics (Section 4), including the softening effect of T and RH on the encapsulation \n\nmodulus.  The model provides a more fundamental understandin g of  the effects of the  environment on \n\nencapsulation debonding.  The techniques and model can be used to develop accelerated testing protocols, \n\nevaluate the adoption of new encapsulations, and make long -term reliability predictions. \n\n# 2. MATERIALS AND METHODS \n\n2.1. Encapsulation  SCB debond specimens \n\nIn order to fabricate representative encapsulation specimens, a 0.45 -mm layer of PV encapsulation (EVA or \n\nPVB) was laminated between a 5.5 -mm -thick glass substrate (low iron -containing, soda -lime  fl oat glass, as \n\nin  the  PV industry)  and a 3.17 -mm -thick titanium plate (6 Al -4 V, Grade -5), as shown in Figure 1a. Prior to \n\nlamination,  the surfaces of the Ti plate and the glass (Sn -de fi cient side)  were carefully cleaned, including \n\nbuf fi ng with pumice  powder, washing with a n aqueous detergent (Billco, Billco  Manufacturing Inc.), rinsing \n\nwith deionized water, and rinsing with isopropyl alcohol. The lamination step was performed in a \n\ncommercial instrument (Model LM -404,  Astropower Inc.) at 145° C for 8 min  under applied pressur e of 1 \n\natm. During lamination, the specimen components  were  fi xed relative to one another using Kapton tape and \n\nwere surrounded with a glass frame to improve thickness  uniformity at the edges. Two parallel cuts (12 -mm \n\napart)  were made through the full thic kness of the Ti plate and the  encapsulation layer with a high -speed \n\ntungsten carbide saw  and a razor blade, respectively (Figure 1b) .\n\nThe elastic beam material (Ti here) was chosen to  maximize elastic cantilever de fl ection  Δ y (m), as given by \n\nwhere  l, h,σy and  εy are the length, thickness, yield stress,  and yield strain of the beam, respectively, and  Gc is \n\nthe encapsulation debond energy, which is unknown before the  debond measurement, but is independent of \n\nbeam material . To maximize Δ y, we calculated the product  σyεy2 for several  candidate beam materials (in \n\nMPa), including steel (0.023),  Al (0.019), poly(methyl methacrylate) (0.039) and Ti  (0.047). We used Ti \n\nbeams in this study but some polymer  beams, such as poly(methy l methacrylate), can be sometimes easier \n\nto bond to the organic PV encapsulation[24]. \n\n2.2. SCB debond test metrology \n\nThe SCB specimen described in Section 3.1 was  fi xe d to a  rigid testing table with screw clamps. A loading tab \n\nwith a  ruby bearing was bonde d to one end of the Ti beam, and  the tab was then connected to an adhesion \n\ntest system  (Delaminator, DTS, Menlo Park, CA) consisting of a linear  actuator (displacem ent -controlled, 0.1 \n\nμm resolution) in  series with a high -resolution mechanical load cell  fi xed to  a high -stiffness frame. The \n\ndisplacement of the linear actuator and the mechanical load were monitored every 0.1 s. \n\nThe actuator was retracted (developing  a tensile force,  P) to  initiate encapsulation debonding, until the \n\ndebond  length (distance from the loading tab to the debondingtip) was ~15 mm. The Ti beam in this \n\ncon fi guration can  be treated as a single cantilever beam  fi xed at the  debonding -tip [39]. T he mechanical \n\ncomplianc e of the  SCB specimen (40 –60  μm/N) was much larger than that  of the testing system (~0.5 \n\nμm/N), which allowed for  accurate debond energy measurements. A minimum beam  thickness (~3 mm here) \n\nwas required to avoid plastic  deformation of  the beam due to bending stress during the  debonding \n\nexperiment. \n\nThe SCB specimen was mechanically loaded at a  constant displacement rate to propagate debonding for a few additional millimeters and then immediately unloaded  to prevent further debonding.  The \n\nloading -unloading  process was repeated for several  cycles at increasing  debond -lengths, adjusting the \n\nactuator displacement rate  to keep the local deformation rate (at the debonding -tip)  constant (refer to \n\nSection 3.5). At each debonding cycle,  the mechanical compliance (displacement per unit load)  of the  SCB \n\nspecimen,  C, and the mechanical load at the  onset of debonding,  Pc (where the load -displacement curve \n\nbecomes non -linear) were recorded. \n\nThe debond driving -force,  G (J m -2), can be than ca lculated with the following expression [40]: \n\nwhere  b is the w idth of the Ti beam and  a is the debondlength. The encapsulation debond energy,  Gc, is then \n\nde fi ned as the value of  G at the onset of debonding, where  P = P c.\n\nThe value of  C for the SCB s pecimen is also a function  of the encapsulation stiffness, which dep ends on  T and \n\nthe  mechanical loading -rate [41]. Using the DTS system  described in Section 3.3, the value of  C (m/N) was \n\nmeasured at selected values of  a at 20 and 50° C in a chamber  of cont rolled RH (38%). The compliance data \n\nwere then  fi tted to the complia nce equation for a cantilever beam with  elastic foundation [42]: \n\nwhere  E, h, and  G are the Young ’s modulus, thickness, and  shear modulus of the Ti beam; and  B is an \n\nencapsulationdependent parameter given by \n\nwhere  t, ν, and  EE are the thickness, Poisson ’s ratio, and  modulus of the encapsulation, respectively. In the \n\ncase of  very large  a or very thin elastic beams ( h/a ≪ 1),  Eqn 3  takes the form of the compliance equation for \n\nthe elastic  beam in cantilever without encap sulation [43] present,  where  C = 4 a3/( Ebh 3). \n\nMaximizing elastic beam de fl ection (thinnest beam)  reduces the contribution of the encapsulation layer to \n\nthe  overall SCB compliance, improves the sensitivity of the  debond experiment, and reduces the size of th e\n\ndamage  zone ahead of the debond -tip. We therefore selected the  smallest possible value of  h (3.17 mm here) \n\nfor the Ti  beam —corresponding to a maximum bend ing stress of  ~70% the yield strength of Ti. \n\n2.3. SCB debond test displacement rate \n\nThe debond -tip  displacement rate,  δ_ , which characterizes  the deformation rates at the debond -tip, was \n\nkept constant  at every debonding cycle to minimize rate -effects in the  debond energy measurement. To keep \n\nδ_ constant, we  derived an equation for the required actuator  displacement  rate ( Δ_ ) as a function of  a as \n\nfollows.  The debond -tip opening displacement, δ, is given by where  ε y is the yield strain of the encapsulation. (At  debonding,  δ = δc and  G = Gc.) \n\nSubstituting Eqns 2 and 3 into the time -derivative of  Eqn 5,  and neglecting second -order and higher -order \n\nterms, the actuator displacement rate  Δ_ (m/s) required  to keep  δ_ constant (at debonding) at increasing \n\ndebondlengths can be approximated as \n\nwhere  Δ_ 0 is the reference actuator displacement rate (at a  refer ence debond -length  a0). To illustrate the use \n\nof this  equation, we calculated the value of  δ_ (at debonding) as a  function of  a, using Eqns 2, 3, and 5 with a \n\nrepresentative  SCB specimen (Figure 2). The values of  δ_ and  a were normalized to the initial val ues,  δ0̇ and \n\na0 = 15  mm, respectively. The value of  δ_ decreases rapidly with  a when the  SCB is loaded at a constant \n\ndisplacement rate ( Δ_ 0) but remains approximately constant ( δ_=δ_ 0≈1) when loaded at the  corrected \n\ndisplacement rate ( Δ_ ) given by Eqn 6 .\n\nIn thinner and st iffer encapsulation layers, where  tE / EE ≪ h, or when the debond experiment starts at large \n\ninitial debond -lengths ( h0.75 /a0 ≪ 1), Eqn 6 reduces to \n\nwhich has been previously used to determine the actuator  displacement rates in debond e xperiments with \n\nthinner  and stiffer  fi lms [10,13] \n\n2.4. Debond growth rate kinetics \n\nA detailed explanation of the load -relaxation method for  debond growth measurement  is provided \n\nelsewhere  [12,39]; a brief summary is given here. To measure the  encapsulation  debond growth rate, d a/ dt,\n\nas a function of  G, the SCB specimens were mechanically loaded in the  DTS adhesion test system (Section 3.2) \n\nup to a value of  G close to t he encapsulation debond energy. The load  relaxation was then recorded as a \n\nfunction of tim e for about  24 h at a  fi xed actuator displacement. Post -experiment  analysis of the \n\nload -relaxation data determined the debond  growth rates, d a/ dt, over the range of ~ 10 8 to ~10 4 m s 1 as a \n\nfunction of  G. The experiments were performed on new  encapsulation s pecimens in an environmental \n\nchamber at  selected values of RH (10, 25, 40, 55, and 70%) and  T (20, 30, 40, and 50° C). The load cell and \n\nactuator were  allowed to equil ibrate in the chamber environment for  6–8 h before each experiment. \n\nThe debond path in the  debond growth experiments  shifted occasionally from the EVA/glass to the EVA/Ti \n\ninterface (and vice versa), which limited the yield of the  debond growth experiments described in the \n\npreceding paragraphs. In a separate set of experiments, a temperature gra dient  was established on th e EVA layer, which successfully  stabilized the debond path at the warmer EVA/glass interface. To establish the  T\n\ngradient, the glass side of the SCB  specimen was heated on a  “hot -plate ” at a  fi xed temperature  (48, 53, or \n\n58° C) an d the Ti side was expose d to the colder  air of an environmental chamber (20, 24, or 30° C). The \n\nheat -fl ow required to maintain this temperature gradient  (4.1 kJm 2) was ~4 times that of solar illumination \n\n(1 kJm 2) as calculated with a one -dimension Fourier h eat  fl ow model using the rmal conductivities of 22, 1, \n\nand  0.23W m 1 K1 for Ti, glass, and EVA [44], respectively.  The purpose of this experiment was not to emulate \n\nfi eldconditions but to investigate the effect of a temperature gradient on stabilizing the de bonding path to \n\nthe EVA/ glass interface. The temperatures at the glass/EVA (28.4, 32.5, and  38° C) and EVA/Ti (20.3, 24.4, \n\nand 30.3° C) were also  calculated with this model. The surface of the hotplate and  the sides of the SCB \n\nspecimen were covered with a 5 -mm  sheet of polystyrene  foam to minimize heat loss. The hot  plate, SCB \n\nspecimen, and the delaminator system were  allowed to stabilize in the environmental chamber for one  hour \n\nto establish the  T gradient across the glass/EVA/Ti  structure. Once the gradient  of ~8° C was established , the \n\ndebond growth experiment described in the preceding paragraph was conducted. \n\n2.5. Tensile specimens \n\nThe SCB mechanical compliance and optimal SCB  testing displacement rate (employed to characterize d a/ dt\n\nand  G c) depend on the  value of the encapsulation modulus  (E)  as described in Sections 3.2 and 3.3. In order \n\nto fabricate  tensile experiments for module measurement, a 0.45 -mm  layer of EVA was laminated between \n\ntwo glass substrates.  Several 3.5 -mm square specimen s were then cut  from the  laminated structure with a \n\nhigh -speed precision saw to make  glass/EVA/glass tensile specimens (3.5 × 3.5 × 10.5 mm). To  measure the \n\nvalue of  E of the EVA layer, a loading tab was  attached to each glass substrate and a monotonic loa d was \n\napplied f rom 0.5 to +0.5MPa in an environmental chamber of controlled relative humidity (38%). The \n\nmodulus  was measured at selected values of  T in recently laminated  specimens (10 days) and in specimens \n\naged for 1 year in a  laboratory environment (23 °C,  fl uorescent  indoor illumination of ~200 lux, and RH \n\nranging from 25 to 45%). The  cross -section area of the tensile specimen corresponded to a  mixed state of \n\nplane -stress (edges) and plane -strain (center)  in the EVA layer. \n\n# 3. RESULTS AND DISCUSSION \n\n3.1. Environmental  sensitivity of EVA  modulus \n\nThe elastic modulus of the EVA encapsulation ( E) as a  function of testing temperature,  T, is shown in Figure \n\n3 for recently laminated (10 days) and laboratory -aged  (1 year) specimens. Aging was conducted at 2 3° C, \n\nunder  fl uorescen t indoor illumination of ~200 lux, and 25 to  45% RH. The storage modulus of EVA,  EDMA ,\n\nmeasured  with dynamic mechanical analysis (DMA) in a previous  experiment by one of the authors [45], is \n\nalso included.  The value of  E and  EDMA  decreased exponentially wi th  T in the entropy -elastic temperature \n\nrange (30 –60° C) below  the melting temperature (65° C),  Tm. The value of  E increased ~10 -fold with aging, \n\nwhich  was attributed to  thermal and possible photochemical cross -linking of EVA  [4] although the aged \n\nspecimens  were exposed only to  indoor illumination ( fl uorescent). To explain, recently  laminated EVA \n\nencapsulation may still contain as much  as 40% of the init ial peroxide used to facilitate cross -linking  [47,48]. \n\nIn  fi elded PV modules, the combination of residual  peroxide and UV radiation explains the cross -linking of \n\nEVA to up to ~90% gel content. \n\nThe Arrhenius equation was  fi tted to the inverse of the  meas ured moduli ( E1 and  EDMA 1) as shown in Figure \n\n3 in dotted lines ( T < Tm), with an activation energy of ~37  kJ  mol 1 in the new specimens and 40kJ mol 1 in \n\nthe aged  specimens, respectively. The similarity between the  activation energies suggests that aging does \n\nnot affect signi fi cantly the temperature -dependence of the compliance  over the one year age. Similarly, the \n\nmelting temperature,  Tm (65° C) [41] did not change signi fi cantly. At  T > Tm, where the polymer is in th e melt \n\nstate, the values of  EDMA  (1.1 MPa) and  E (0.7 and 4 MPa) were independent of  T.3.2. Mechanical compliance of SCB debond  specimen \n\nThe mechanical  compliance,  C, of the SCB specimen as a  function of debond length ( a) is shown in Figure 4 \n\nfor  EVA (circle data points) and PVB (square data points)  specimens, at two selected values of  T. The \n\ncompliance of  the bare Ti cantilever beam is also shown for re ference as  a solid line. The value of  C increased \n\nwith  a in the SC B specimens and in the bare Ti beam. The specimen with  the lowest encapsulation storage \n\nmodulus, PVB ( G′~0.4  to 40 MPa) [49], exhibited the largest SCB compliance,  followed by the specimen with \n\nEVA (G ′~20 to  1000 MPa) [41], followed by the bare Ti beam. The  value of  C increased with  T as a \n\nconsequence of the  temperature -dependence of the encapsulation modul us;  this dependence was stronger \n\nin the more compliant  encapsulation (PVB) \n\nTo obtain an analytical exp ression of  C as a function of  a, we  fi tted Eqn 3 to the compliance data of the SCB \n\nspecimens with EVA (dashed -line) and PVB (dotted -line),  as shown in Fig ure 4. The  fi tted equations were \n\nthen used  to calculate the encapsulation debond energy (Eqn 2) as  des cribed in Section 3.2. \n\n3.3. Encapsulation debond energy \n\nThe measured debond energy of the EVA/glass interface,  G c, decreased from 2.15 to 1.8 kJ m 2 in th e 25 to \n\n50° C temperature range (Figure 5). At temperatures close to  Tm (65° C), the value of  Gc decreased six -fold to \n\n0.3 kJ m 2. The value of  G c remained constant in the melt state at  any  T, similar to the modulus in Figure 3. \n\nThe debond energy of the PVB/ Ti interface, shown in  Figure 5 (circular data points), also decreased with  T\n\nin  the 20 to 40° C temperature range. The values of  Gc for  PVB/Ti (0.67 to 0.52 kJ m 2) were much lower than \n\nthose  of EVA/glass in the same temperature range, which can be  attribut ed to the lower yield stress of PVB \n\nand to the nature  of the attachment at the  interface. EVA is typically bonded  to glass covalently, as enabled \n\nthrough a silane chemistry  (primer) typically present in the EVA formulation[27]. In  contrast, PVB is more \n\nwea kly bonded via hydrogen  bonding [27]. \n\nTo better understand the effect of te mperature on  debond energy, Eqn 5 can be solved for  Gc (J m 2) [9]: \n\nwhere  E(t, T) is the temperature -dependent modulus of the  material at the debonding -tip. \n\nIn the EVA/glass interf ace, the extent of plastic deformation during debonding is determined  "

/**
 * Playwright e2e config for POLARIS frontend.
 *
 * Tests assume both servers are already running (frontend on
 * SCREENSHOT_BASE_URL, backend on POLARIS_V6_BACKEND_URL). Phase 0 ships the
 * tests; CI wiring (auto-spawn servers + run on push) lands when the v6 dev
 * cluster is live (Task 0.3).
 *
 * Run locally:
 *   1. Terminal A: PYTHONPATH=src python -m uvicorn polaris_v6.api.app:app --port 8000
 *   2. Terminal B: cd web && npx next start -p 3738
 *   3. Terminal C: cd web && SCREENSHOT_BASE_URL=http://127.0.0.1:3738 npx playwright test
 */

  testDir: "./tests/e2e",

  // Linux baselines are generated.
